name: Irish
notes: Irish (Gaeilge). ~1.7M speakers (2016 census; ~80K daily L1). PERVASIVE BROAD/SLENDER DISTINCTION: every consonant has two versions (velarised 'broad' vs palatalised 'slender'), and the flanking vowel LETTERS encode which — 'caol le caol agus leathan le leathan'. Many of those flanking vowels are pure quality markers and are not pronounced (⟨e⟩ before ⟨a o⟩; ⟨i⟩ next to a back vowel), which is why they map to the empty string in positional_graphemes. Initial mutation: lenition (séimhiú) and eclipsis (urú, encoded as the digraphs mb/gc/nd/bp/dt/bhf/ts). Svarabhakti epenthesis is modelled in allophone_rules. Three main dialects: Munster, Connacht, Ulster — the spec targets the Connacht/Ulster initial-stress type. An Caighdeán Oifigiúil = written standard. Refs: Ó Siadhail (1989), Ní Chiosáin (1999), Wikipedia 'Irish orthography'/'Irish phonology'.

sources.author: Ó Siadhail, M.
sources.title: Modern Irish: Grammatical Structure and Dialectal Variation

sources.author: Ní Chiosáin, M.
sources.title: Syllables and Phonotactics in Irish
sources.notes: Standard treatment of Irish syllable structure, the broad/slender (velarised/palatalised) contrast and svarabhakti epenthesis. Not fetched; no page verified.

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Irish orthography
sources.notes: Accessible overview; source of the statement that '⟨e⟩ before ⟨a⟩ or ⟨o⟩ and ⟨i⟩ bordering either side of ⟨u⟩ are silent, but mark a slender consonant', of the caol-le-caol rule, of the vowel digraph table (⟨ea⟩ /a/, ⟨ai⟩ /a/, ⟨ei⟩ /ɛ/, ⟨io⟩ /ɪ/, ⟨oi⟩ /ɛ ɔ/, ⟨ui⟩ /ɪ/, ⟨eo⟩ /oː/, ⟨iu⟩ /ʊ/, ⟨ao⟩ /iː eː/, ⟨ae⟩ /eː/, ⟨aí oí uí⟩ /iː/, ⟨ia⟩ /iə/, ⟨ua⟩ /uə/), and of the eclipsis digraphs mb gc nd bp dt bhf ts.

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Irish phonology
sources.notes: Accessible overview; source of the consonant inventory (/bˠ bʲ pˠ pʲ mˠ mʲ fˠ fʲ d̪ˠ dʲ t̪ˠ tʲ sˠ ʃ k c ɡ ɟ x ç ɣ j l̪ˠ lˠ lʲ n̪ˠ nˠ nʲ ɾˠ ɾʲ/), of unstressed-vowel reduction to /ə/, and of the svarabhakti epenthesis rule.

allophone_rules.notes: Svarabhakti (epenthesis): in Irish an unwritten short /ə/ is inserted between a preceding ⟨l n r⟩ and a following ⟨b bh ch f g mh⟩ inside a single morpheme after a SHORT vowel — gorm /ˈɡɔɾˠəmˠ/, dearg /ˈdʲaɾˠəɡ/, leanbh /ˈl̠ʲanˠəw/, borb /ˈbˠɔɾˠəbˠ/, ainm /ˈanʲəmʲ/ (Wikipedia, 'Irish phonology' §Epenthesis; Ó Siadhail 1989). Modelled here as a phoneme→surface rewrite that appends the epenthetic vowel to the sonorant. LIMITATION: the engine's rule vocabulary cannot see vowel length in the preceding nucleus, so the blocking of epenthesis after a long vowel or diphthong (téarma /ˈtʲeːɾˠmˠə/) and across a morpheme boundary is NOT captured; those words are over-generated.

stress.notes: Irish (Connacht/Ulster) stresses the FIRST syllable (Wikipedia, 'Irish phonology' §Prosody); unstressed short vowels reduce to /ə/. Munster instead attracts stress to a long vowel or diphthong in the 2nd/3rd syllable and is NOT modelled here.
